title: Best VPNs for Torrenting in 2026
description: Torrenting without a VPN exposes your IP to the entire swarm. These are the VPNs built for P2P with no throttling, reliable leak protection, and zero-logging policies that hold up in court.
content:
Torrenting without protection exposes your IP address to everyone in the swarm. Your ISP sees every torrent you download. Copyright holders and law enforcement use torrent monitoring to identify and sue P2P users. A VPN masks your real IP, encrypts your traffic, and prevents your ISP from knowing what you're downloading. But not all VPNs are suitable for torrenting. Many explicitly block P2P, others throttle speeds, and some keep logs that can be subpoenaed. This guide covers VPNs that are actually built for torrenting, tested in 2026.

ProtonVPN is the gold standard for torrenting. It allows P2P on all servers, has no speed throttling, uses strong encryption (AES-256), and maintains a strict no-logging policy that has been tested in court. ProtonVPN is based in Switzerland, outside the reach of US and EU surveillance directives, and the company has publicly refused to hand over user logs to law enforcement. Speeds are fast enough for 4K streaming and large torrent downloads. The only catch is price: ProtonVPN starts at $10 per month (billed annually). The premium plan allows 10 simultaneous connections, which is useful if you torrent on multiple devices.

Mullvad is a privacy-focused VPN built by people who care deeply about anonymity, not profit maximization. Mullvad allows P2P on all servers, does not keep any logs, and even deletes your VPN account after 12 months of inactivity (you don't have to remember to delete it yourself). You don't create an account with a username or password; instead, Mullvad assigns you a random 44-digit account number. No email required, no payment history tied to your name. If you're serious about anonymity, Mullvad is unmatched. Speed is decent but sometimes slower than ProtonVPN depending on your region. Cost is transparent: $5 per month, no discounts, no upsells.

NordVPN allows P2P on all servers and is one of the most popular VPNs globally. Speed is good, the app is user-friendly, and it includes built-in threat protection. The no-logging policy has been independently audited. However, NordVPN is more expensive at $12 per month (with annual commitment) and has had some security hiccups in the past (most resolved). For casual torrenting, NordVPN is reliable. For heavy-duty privacy, ProtonVPN or Mullvad are better choices.

Surfshark allows P2P on all servers and costs $3.99 per month on an annual plan, making it one of the cheapest premium options. Speed is solid, and you get unlimited simultaneous connections, which is a real advantage if you torrent on many devices. The no-logging policy is audited. Surfshark is newer than the competitors on this list (founded 2018 vs. ProtonVPN's 2014), so less battle-tested in court, but has a good reputation so far.

What to avoid: ExpressVPN, while fast and popular, is not recommended for torrenting. ExpressVPN does not allow P2P on most of its servers and explicitly bans torrenting in its terms of service. Windscribe and TunnelBear both block or throttle P2P traffic. Free VPNs are almost never suitable for torrenting because free providers either have no resources for the bandwidth torrenting requires or sell logs to make money. Even VPN services that claim to allow P2P but run on cloud infrastructure shared with non-torrent users often impose hidden throttling if they detect high bandwidth usage.

IP and DNS leaks are a critical issue. Even if you're connected to a VPN, a misconfiguration can leak your real IP or allow DNS queries to reveal your identity. The best VPNs for torrenting use multi-hop routing (routing your traffic through two VPN servers instead of one), have built-in leak detection, and use their own DNS servers instead of default ISP DNS. ProtonVPN and Mullvad both excel here. Before torrenting, test your VPN with a tool like ipleak.net or dnsleaktest.com to confirm your real IP is hidden.

Port forwarding is useful for torrenting because it can improve upload speeds and peer connectivity. ProtonVPN and Mullvad both offer port forwarding, which is rare among VPN providers. Surfshark does not. If you're torrenting large files frequently, port forwarding can measurably improve performance.

Jurisdiction matters. VPNs based in privacy-friendly countries (Switzerland, Iceland, Panama) are more resilient to legal requests for logs. ProtonVPN (Switzerland) and Mullvad (Sweden) have both publicly refused government requests. NordVPN (Panama) and Surfshark (Virgin Islands) are also based in favorable jurisdictions but are younger companies with less tested track records in court.

Kill switch is essential. If your VPN connection drops unexpectedly, your torrent client will continue uploading without VPN protection, exposing your IP. A kill switch automatically stops all internet traffic if the VPN drops. ProtonVPN, Mullvad, NordVPN, and Surfshark all include this feature.

For maximum privacy while torrenting, ProtonVPN or Mullvad are your best bets. ProtonVPN if you want ease of use and support, Mullvad if you want maximum anonymity. For budget-conscious torrenting, Surfshark offers good value with fast speeds. Avoid free VPNs, avoid VPN services that block P2P, and always test for leaks before your first torrent.
